questions.prompt: Why does offset pagination perform badly on deep pages?

choices.text: `OFFSET 1000000` makes the database scan and discard 1M rows before returning the next 20. The cost is O(offset), not O(1) — performance degrades linearly with depth. On a 100M-row table, page 5000 is unusably slow even with proper indexes. Cursor pagination fixes this: the cursor is the indexed value of the last returned row; the next query is `WHERE id > $cursor LIMIT 20` — an index lookup, constant time regardless of depth. Stripe, GitHub, Twitter API all use cursor pagination for exactly this reason. Trade-off: lose 'jump to page N' UX (acceptable for feeds, infinite scroll, sequential consumers; not great for admin tables where users expect page numbers). For large datasets and any sequential-consumer API: cursors. For small datasets or admin UIs: offset is fine. Knowing the trade-off lets you pick consciously rather than defaulting to offset everywhere and hitting performance walls at scale.

questions.explanation: OFFSET scans and discards N rows. Linear with depth. Cursors are O(1) via index lookup.

questions.prompt: API returns `{"data": [...], "total": 1234567}`. Pagination is slow. Likely cause?

choices.text: The `COUNT(*)` to compute total is the slow part — on a million-row table, even with indexes, COUNT scans matching rows and tallies. Combined with the pagination query, every request does the expensive count. Three fixes: (1) drop the total — many modern APIs don't include it; use `has_more` boolean from 'fetched limit+1 rows, last is dropped, has_more = was there an extra'; (2) cached approximate total — Postgres `reltuples` or precomputed stats updated periodically; (3) only compute total when the client asks (`?include_total=true`) — most clients don't need it. UX implication: 'Page X of Y' becomes 'Load more.' For most APIs in 2026 this is fine; users navigate feeds and infinite-scroll, not page numbers. If exact total is genuinely needed (admin reports, billing), do it as a separate endpoint that explicitly takes the count cost. Default: omit total from list endpoints.

questions.explanation: COUNT(*) is the slow part. Drop the total or compute it lazily / approximately.
